schools.summary: Henry: the temple without the ark is a body without a soul; bringing in the ark is the one thing needful, and Solomon presides as David did.
Keil and Delitzsch lay out the chapter's three sections; Ellicott distinguishes the elders from the heads of tribes and notes the dedication blended with Tabernacles.
Gill queries whether a sanhedrim yet existed among the summoned elders.
Clarke, following Usher, dates the deferred dedication to a jubilee year.
Ellicott reads Ethanim as 'month of gifts' (Tisri) and the Feast of Tabernacles as the most joyful, here doubled to fourteen days; Keil and Delitzsch derive the name from the flowing brooks.
Gill distinguishes the feast of dedication from Tabernacles, both lasting fourteen days.
Clarke identifies the feast simply as Tabernacles in the seventh month.

debates.question: What does the month-name Ethanim mean?

sides.position: 'month of the flowing brooks', from the autumn rains

schools.summary: Ellicott: bearing the ark was the Kohathites' duty, but bringing it from the sanctuary was the priests' alone; Keil and Delitzsch identify the tent as the Mosaic tabernacle from Gibeon.
Gill notes only that the elders came to Zion, the city of David.
Ellicott traces the Kohathite, Gershonite, and Merarite division and the disused tabernacle stored as a relic; Keil and Delitzsch note priests and Levites bore up the holy things.
Clarke supposes the Gibeon tabernacle was brought in and David's temporary one demolished.
Gill marks only the route from the city of David to the temple.

debates.question: Which tabernacle was brought into the temple?

sides.position: the Gibeon tabernacle, David's tent being demolished

schools.summary: Ellicott sets the innumerable sacrifice against David's when the ark came to Zion; Keil and Delitzsch fix the offering 'before the ark' in the temple court.
Gill binds king and people into one worshipping body around the ark.
Henry: a rich and cheerful people fitly consecrated their wealth, for public mercies call for public acknowledgments.
Keil and Delitzsch describe the ark set in the oracle under the cherubim, the wings shading even its poles.
Gill notes the place destined for the ark, as it had had in the tabernacle.
Henry: the most holy place, made so by the ark, is the appointed rest after all the ark's wanderings, under the protection of angels.
Keil and Delitzsch: the outspread wings shaded the ark and its poles, indicating their orientation.
Gill: the cherubim spread their wings over the most holy place, from wall to wall.
Keil and Delitzsch: the verb means the poles 'were long', not that new poles were made; they were left visible to direct the high priest.
Gill: the staves were drawn out a little, not lengthened nor removed, the ark now having a fixed place.
Clarke: 'unto this day' proves the book was written before the first temple's destruction.

debates.question: Were the carrying-poles lengthened or new ones made?

sides.position: no — they were simply long and left drawn out
new, longer poles were made (Kimchi, Ben Gersom)

schools.summary: Ellicott: the emphasis that only the tables were 'in' the ark determines the reading of Hebrews 9:4; the hallowed granite tables bind religion and morality, God known in his moral nature.
Gill, with Ben Gersom, sets the rod and manna beside, not in, the ark.
Clarke refers the crux to his note on Hebrews 9:4.

debates.question: How does this bear on 'wherein' in Hebrews 9:4?

sides.position: no stress on 'wherein'; the rod and manna were beside the ark

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the cloud, symbol of God's presence, fills the temple as it filled the tabernacle; Ellicott traces the Shechinah from Sinai through Ezekiel's departing glory to Haggai's messianic promise.
Clarke marks the timing — the cloud came after the ark was set in the holy of holies, before any service began.
Keil and Delitzsch: the cloud did not drive the priests out but they could not minister; Ellicott sets their shrinking among Isaiah's and the apostles' awe — reverence, not mere terror.
Gill offers darkness, dazzling light, or terror as the cause of their inability.
Clarke: the cloud filled the whole temple, court and all, so Solomon knew God had honoured the place.
Henry: the thick darkness is a token of favour, not displeasure; God who dwells in light dwells with men in darkness because they cannot bear his brightness.
Clarke grounds the saying in the cloud of the wilderness and the tabernacle.
Ellicott defends 'thick darkness' against Thenius's conjecture 'Jerusalem'; Keil and Delitzsch refer the words to God's promised presence in the cloud.

sides.position: 'thick darkness' — supported by Hebrew, Vulgate, and Josephus

schools.summary: Henry casts the verse as Solomon giving God legal possession of the house — 'Surely I come,' says God; 'Amen, even so, come, Lord,' says Solomon.
Gill: assured by the cloud, Solomon turns to address the Lord whose habitation the house will be.
Keil and Delitzsch and Clarke: the emphatic 'surely' and 'for ever' mark Solomon's settled conviction that God has accepted the place.
Gill and Ellicott describe the king turning from the temple to the people on the brazen scaffold of 2 Chronicles 6:13.
Clarke supplies the likely Aaronic blessing and Solomon's acting the high priest's part, after Moses and David.
Henry: the people stood up to receive the blessing in reverence and readiness.
Henry: the blessing begins with blessing God; we feel mercies most when we trace them to the promise and compare God's word with his works.
Gill hears the covenant God afresh owning Israel by dwelling among them.
Keil and Delitzsch join God's 'mouth' and 'hand' as the twin pillars of the doxology.
Clarke and Ellicott correct the verse from the fuller text of 2 Chronicles 6:5-6, restoring the choice of Jerusalem; the parallel of chosen place and chosen person is exact.
Gill reckons the exodus some 488 years past, gathering the sweep of God's dealings.
Henry: God chose the person, then put the temple into David's heart; the design was the father's though the deed was withheld.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the words to 2 Samuel 7 and note God approves the intention as such.
Gill: David's mind was disposed and his heart set upon the work.
Henry: sincere intentions to do good are accepted though providence prevents their execution — the desire of a man is his kindness.
Keil and Delitzsch render the divine commendation 'thou didst well that it was in thy mind.'
Gill notes the word came to David by Nathan the prophet.
Henry: God reserved the honour of building for the son, denying it to the father without rejecting him.
Gill: the refusal is implied in the earlier word through Nathan.
Keil and Delitzsch connect the naming of the son-builder to 2 Samuel 7:12-13.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'Jehovah has set up His word' explains 'fulfilled with his hand'; the ark is the kernel and star of the temple.
Gill fixes the reference to the promise of the son's building the temple.
Henry: whatever good we do is the performance of God's promise to us; the more we do the more we owe.

themes: the ark the star of the temple

schools.summary: Clarke reads the tables as the 'sign' of the covenant, as the cup signs Christ's blood; Ellicott expounds the divine covenants as proceeding from God's will and free grace, not reciprocal bargains.
Gill marks the place set for the ark as the most holy place.
Henry: the king prays himself, kneeling with spread hands, a type of Christ the great intercessor, opening the house as a house of prayer for all people.
Clarke confirms the brazen scaffold and shows the lifted hands to be a universal posture of prayer, with heathen parallels.
Gill: the altar was typical of Christ, through whom all prayer becomes acceptable.
Keil and Delitzsch note from v.54 that the whole prayer was offered on his knees.

themes: house of prayer for all people

schools.summary: Ellicott traces 'there is no God like thee' and 'keepest covenant and mercy' to the Red Sea song, David's thanksgiving, and the Decalogue, marking Israel's training toward monotheism; Keil and Delitzsch make the confession the ground of confident prayer.
Gill: the covenant God is thereby distinguished from all the gods of the Gentiles.
Henry: performance is the crown of promise; fresh experiences of God's faithfulness call for enlarged praises.
Keil and Delitzsch bind God's 'mouth' and 'hand'; Clarke refers the kept promise to 2 Samuel 7:13, now literally fulfilled.
Gill names the content — the promise of a son to build the temple.
Henry: former mercies encourage us to plead the promise; Solomon repeats it without omitting the condition, learning from David how to pray.
Keil and Delitzsch note the conditional form matching 1 Kings 2:4 and 6:12, with the divine rejection more distinctly indicated.
Henry: God's promises are the guide of our desires and the ground of our hopes in prayer.
Keil and Delitzsch: this verse introduces the prayers that follow and refers to the whole promise of 2 Samuel 7:12-16, mercy not withdrawn even when the seed sins.
Gill glosses the plea — let thy word be truly made good and punctually performed.
Henry: humble admiration of God's condescension and acknowledgment of the house's incapacity — infinite distance between us and him.
Gill turns the wonder toward Christ's condescension to tabernacle in flesh, the temple a figure of his body.
Keil and Delitzsch: the verse meets contracted ideas of God's presence — the house cannot hold him, yet he will hear.
Clarke expands the heavens into systems upon systems, all a speck before the God who inhabits eternity.
Henry: a humble, earnest, believing prayer, resting not on the king's dignity but on his servanthood.
Keil and Delitzsch join the 'yet' to the optative of v.26 — the confession of God's infinity deepens rather than breaks the prayer.
Gill marks the royal suppliant owning himself the Lord's servant.
Keil and Delitzsch ground the request in the name God has set there — his gracious presence is the reason his eyes are open.
Henry: the temple is not the object of worship but an instituted medium, typifying the mediation of Christ, the true temple.
Clarke: 'My name shall be there' means God will show forth his saving power to sincere worshippers.
Gill: the eyes open toward the house are toward the people who pray in it.
Henry: heaven is God's true dwelling, of which the temple is a figure; the pardon sought reaches even the iniquity of their holy things.
Clarke: the temple typifies Christ, in whose human nature dwelt the fullness of the Godhead; all prayer must go through him.
Gill ascribes the acceptance of prayer to Christ's blood, righteousness, and mediation.
Keil and Delitzsch note Solomon prayed in the court toward the temple.
Clarke: the first of seven cases — a doubtful charge purged by oath at the altar, Solomon praying that God discover the truth.
Gill instances a breach of trust as the secret wrong.
Henry: the throne of grace becomes a throne of judgment; note the later abuse of swearing by the temple.
Gill: God hears when the injured makes supplication for justice.
Clarke: the case asks that the wicked be condemned and the righteous justified, the truth discovered.
Henry: the sanctuary made a seat of discriminating justice as well as of mercy.
Clarke: the second case — defeat and captivity for sin, met by turning, confessing, and praying, then restoration to the land.
Keil and Delitzsch anchor the case in the covenant threats of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28.
Gill: such defeat had been Israel's case and might be again, even in a time of peace.
Gill: the sin forgiven is public, not personal, the cause of the national calamity.
Keil and Delitzsch keep pardon and homecoming within the covenant — restoration is the visible seal of forgiveness.
Clarke: the third case — drought for sin, the early and latter rain withheld, met by prayer, confession, and turning.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'because thou teachest them the good way' is the very ground on which mercy may be asked.
Gill pictures the shut heaven — a serene sky without a cloud.
Gill: God forgives by removing the drought, pardon and rain coming together.
Keil and Delitzsch: the chastisement is instruction — God gives rain again to a people he has taught.
Henry: God teaches the good way by his Spirit, word, and prophets, so trouble both profits and prepares for deliverance.
Clarke itemises nine evils — famine, pestilence, blasting, mildew, locust, caterpillar, siege, plague, sickness — brought before the Lord by those who know their heart's corruption.
Keil and Delitzsch trace each item to the covenant curses of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28.
Gill recalls the three years' famine of David's time as precedent.
Henry: 'the plague of his own heart' is the inward corruption — sin the spiritual disease that drives a man to the sanctuary.
Keil and Delitzsch note the sweep 'any man, or all thy people Israel', embracing individual and nation alike.
Gill keeps the door open to every distress, named or unnamed.
Henry: the verse appeals to God's omniscience, justice, and mercy at once — he alone knows the heart, and his forgiveness begets a lifelong fear.
Gill: heaven is God's dwelling place more truly than the temple Solomon built.
Gill: God hears and forgives for his goodness' sake, that his people may fear him.
Henry: the experience of answered prayer and pardon should engage us to fear God while we live.
Clarke: the fifth case — heathen coming to become proselytes, received and protected, that God's name be known over the earth.
Keil and Delitzsch note Moses himself allowed the foreigner to sacrifice (Numbers 15:14); the stranger comes drawn by God's name.
Gill describes the stranger as one of another country with a disposition to the true worship of God.
Gill: the great name draws them — God the eternal, self-existent Being displayed in his mighty works.
Keil and Delitzsch: the strong hand and outstretched arm are the standing expression for God's wonders in guiding Israel, now the magnet for the nations.
Henry: Solomon prays the stranger's prayer be granted the more freely, that all people fear God as Israel — one gospel for native and stranger alike.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'the name called over this house' denotes the acknowledgment of God's working within it, not merely a title.
Gill notes simply that God is to hear the stranger's prayer.
Gill gives the just occasion of war; Keil and Delitzsch read the sixth case as a war 'by the appointment of God', defending the passage's authenticity.
Clarke restricts such wars to divine appointment, holding that none but defensive wars are lawful under the Christian dispensation.
Henry: soldiers must pray for themselves, for praying should go along with fighting.

outliers.note: Insists no such divinely-appointed wars exist under the gospel — 'all other wars are diabolic' save the merely defensive.

schools.summary: Gill: the prayer is for success; to maintain their cause is to grant victory to a people fighting at God's command.
Keil and Delitzsch pair the righteous war of this case with the just captivity of the next, both praying toward the temple from afar.
Clarke reads 'there is no man that sinneth not' as 'may not sin' — no man impeccable — and warns against making the text a stronghold for denying redemption from sin in this life.
Keil and Delitzsch keep the seventh case within the exile-threat of Leviticus 26, extended to every transportation.
Gill: the same people who went out to battle now fail to keep the divine commands.

debates.question: Does 'there is no man that sinneth not' assert universal sin or only its possibility?

sides.position: only the possibility — 'no man that may not sin', none impeccable
the universal fact of human sinfulness (the common rendering)

schools.summary: Gill renders 'bethink themselves' as 'return to their heart' — remembering and reflecting on the sins that caused the captivity.
Keil and Delitzsch note the threefold confession was adopted by the Jews in captivity, echoed in Daniel 9:5.
Gill: the return is wholehearted, with great sincerity and uprightness.
Keil and Delitzsch note the multiplied objects of the captive's gaze — the land, the city, the house — giving direction to his prayer.
Gill: God is to hear for their deliverance and to maintain their cause — plead it, do them justice, deliver them.
Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as the pivot from repentance to restoration within the seventh case.
Ellicott shows the prayer singularly fulfilled in Babylon — the favour of Nebuchadnezzar, Cyrus, and the Persian kings; Keil and Delitzsch render 'make them for compassion before their captors'.
Gill: forgiveness is proved by returning them to their land.
Clarke: Solomon prays not for release but that their captors may use them well and keep them a distinct people.
Gill: they are God's chosen people and his inheritance, his portion and possession.
Keil and Delitzsch read the plea as the foundation of the request — Israel may be heard because they are God's own, redeemed from the iron furnace.
Gill glosses 'open' as attentive, covering the king's prayers and the people's, now and hereafter.
Henry: after all the particulars Solomon concludes with the general request that God hearken to all his praying people.
Keil and Delitzsch make the verse, with those around it, the foundation of the whole appeal.
Gill enumerates the means of separation — choice, redemption, peculiar laws, and special blessings.
Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the summary ground of the prayer — Israel heard because God set them apart by his own hand and word.
Keil and Delitzsch confirm from this verse that the whole prayer was offered kneeling; Ellicott records the fire from heaven and second cloud of 2 Chronicles 7:1-3.
Gill reads Solomon interceding before the altar as a type of Christ.

Ellicott notes that blessing was properly the priests' office, yet the king alone is conspicuous, his words being praise, prayer, and exhortation rather than strictly blessing; Keil and Delitzsch ground it in the covenant promises.
Henry: never was a congregation dismissed with words more fitly spoken, pronounced standing, as one having authority.
Gill: Solomon blesses as father and as preacher in Jerusalem.
Clarke: the blessing itself is contained in the verses following.
Henry: Solomon begins by blessing God for rest, and writes a receipt in full — not one word of all his good promise has failed.

Ellicott traces the promise of rest from Sinai through Joshua and David to Solomon the Peaceful, warning that this rest was conditional and would be forfeited; Keil and Delitzsch note it was secured fully by David's victories.

outliers.note: Warns that the rest was conditional on covenant obedience, which Solomon himself broke — 'a gleam of prosperity, soon swallowed up in disaster.'

schools.summary: Henry: the presence of God is all in all; the congregation soon to scatter is dismissed with 'the Lord be present with us.'
Gill: nothing is more desirable than God's presence; Solomon could ask no greater blessing.
Keil and Delitzsch join the verse to the prayer that God incline their hearts.
Henry: the power of grace is the best blessing — our hearts are naturally averse, and only grace, obtained by prayer, inclines them.
Ellicott finds here the co-operation of preventing grace and free responsibility — 'God inclines the heart, yet the heart must yield itself' (Philippians 2:12-13).
Gill reads the inclining as the Spirit's work to love, fear, and serve God.
Henry: what Solomon asks for his prayer is granted in Christ's intercession, before God day and night against the accuser (Revelation 12:10).
Keil and Delitzsch render 'as every day demands' — a plea for perpetual advocacy of king and people.
Clarke notes the supplement and the fuller ending preserved in 2 Chronicles 6:41-42.

themes: the day's matter in its day

schools.summary: Henry: the aim is God's glory in the enlargement of his kingdom — not tribute to Israel but that all may know the Lord is God.
Gill: God is known by chastening, hearing, forgiving, delivering, and maintaining his people's cause.
Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the goal of the blessing, joined to the call for a perfect heart.

themes: all the earth to know God

schools.summary: Henry: evangelical perfection is obedience universal without dividing, upright without dissembling, constant without declining.
Keil and Delitzsch: the 'perfect' heart is the undivided heart, the very standard by which later kings, and Solomon first, would be found wanting.
Clarke: be sincere in faith, irreproachable in conduct.
Gill: fire came down from heaven and consumed the sacrifices, a second confirmation, besides the cloud, of God's acceptance.
Keil and Delitzsch rebut Thenius's objection that the sacrifice numbers are too large, exposing his false assumptions.
Henry: the people had abundant joy while they attended at God's house.
Henry: the heathen offered by hundreds, Solomon by thousands; where God sows plentifully he reaps accordingly — few spend more on their souls than their bodies.
Ellicott accepts the number but warns the profusion may carry the heathen idea, condemned by the prophets, that God is pleased with thousands of rams — in which lurked Israel's later ruin.
Clarke reads the figure as the whole amount over fourteen days.
Gill notes the peace-offerings furnished the people's feast.

outliers.note: Hears in the very lavishness a lurking danger — the heathen notion of buying God's favour, the seed of Israel's later disaster.

schools.summary: Gill: Solomon hallowed the middle of the court for present use, the brazen altar being too small.
Clarke: an altar was erected in the court because the great altar could not hold so many sacrifices.
Henry: those generous in serving God need not stint for want of room and occasion.

Ellicott and Clarke fix the geography — from the entering in of Hamath (Coele-Syria) to the river of Egypt (the torrent at El-Arish, not the Nile), the land's fullest extent; Keil and Delitzsch describe a great assembly from north to south.
Henry: the feast of Tabernacles followed the dedication, fourteen days in all, yet they said not, what a weariness is this.
Gill: the people partook of the peace offerings and the liberal prince's provision.

themes: from Hamath to the river of Egypt

schools.summary: Henry: they went home joyful and glad of heart, God's goodness the matter of their joy; so we should go home rejoicing from holy ordinances, David a type of Christ.
Gill and Keil and Delitzsch note the eighth-day dismissal, the ordered close of the longest festival in Israel's history.
Clarke: a contented, peaceful, happy people — 'but alas! the king fell, and the nation followed his example.'

outliers.note: Lets the eye travel forward from the joy of the hour to its loss — the king's later fall and the nation's decline.
